questions.q: What is a root node in a tree?
questions.a: The top node with no parent.
They give a systematic way to visit every node in a controlled order.

questions.q: When should you use a tree instead of a flat list?
questions.a: When the data is hierarchical, ordered by key, or benefits from fast structured lookup.

questions.q: What invariant does a BST enforce?
questions.a: Left subtree keys are smaller, right subtree keys are larger.

questions.q: Why does in-order traversal of a BST produce sorted output?
questions.a: It visits left, node, then right, which respects the BST ordering invariant.

questions.q: When is a plain BST not enough?
questions.a: When you need worst-case guarantees instead of relying on the tree staying balanced.

questions.q: What balance condition does an AVL tree enforce?
questions.a: Each node has left/right subtree heights differing by at most 1.

questions.q: Why do AVL trees use rotations?
questions.a: Rotations restore balance after inserts or deletes without rebuilding the whole tree.

questions.q: When would you choose AVL over a plain BST?
questions.a: When you want tighter balance and faster lookups at the cost of more rebalancing work.

questions.q: What color-property idea does a red-black tree use?
questions.a: Nodes are colored red or black to keep the tree roughly balanced.

questions.q: Why do red-black trees often rotate less than AVL trees?
questions.a: They allow more relaxed balance, so fewer updates need rebalancing.

questions.q: When is a red-black tree a better fit than AVL?
questions.a: When write-heavy workloads benefit more from fewer rotations than from the tightest possible balance.

questions.q: What structure does a heap maintain?
questions.a: Each parent is ordered relative to its children so the min or max is at the root.

questions.q: Why is a heap good for a priority queue?
questions.a: Insert and extract-min or extract-max both stay logarithmic while preserving fast access to the top priority.

questions.q: When would you use a heap instead of a BST?
questions.a: When you care most about repeatedly pulling the smallest or largest item rather than sorted iteration.

questions.q: What makes a B-tree or B+ tree different from a binary tree?
questions.a: Each node stores multiple keys and has multiple children.

questions.q: Why are B-trees good for databases and file systems?
questions.a: Wide nodes reduce tree height and match disk page sizes, so the structure minimizes I/O.

questions.q: When would you choose a B-tree over a BST or trie?
questions.a: When you need sorted storage on disk, range scans, and predictable page-oriented access.
